Grasp Planning Explorer

An interactive map of ~56 robotic grasp-planning methods · part of the COMPARE ecosystem (robot-manipulation.org)

User & Engineer Manual

1Overview

The Grasp Planning Explorer is a React knowledge-discovery dashboard that turns a corpus of grasp-planning research papers into a navigable, evidence-grounded map of methods. It is built for robotics engineers who need to find, compare, and trust the methods relevant to a problem — without reading every paper end-to-end. You ask questions in natural language; a language model traverses a knowledge graph built from the papers and returns ranked methods, traceable evidence, and benchmark numbers you can verify down to the source table cell.

1. Natural-language query & reasoning. Ask "methods for cluttered scenes with parallel-jaw grippers"; the system detects intent, walks the graph (paper→method→technique→limitation), and synthesizes an answer with a step-by-step proof.
2. Visual similarity map. A UMAP 2D layout (UMAP places similar methods near each other on a 2D map) grouped into automatically detected clusters (HDBSCAN, a density-based clustering method) — so nearby dots mean similar methods. Scatter, network, table, and detail views all cross-highlight.
3. Evidence-graded benchmarks. Leaderboards, head-to-head records, and cross-paper validation, each carrying an A/B/C trust grade (see Section 3), a CV% (coefficient of variation across independent papers; lower = more consistent), and a crop of the source table.
4. Predicted relationships. A heterogeneous graph neural network (a GraphSAGE link predictor with a DistMult decoder) overlays inferred edges with confidence scores to surface non-obvious related work.

2Getting Started & the Four Views

Quick start — your first 30 seconds
  1. Go to https://robot-manipulation.org/grasp-planning (the default route; no login or API key is needed to run a query — only the Admin page is token-gated).
  2. You land on Graph Reasoning.
  3. Type a natural-language question in the search box at the top and press Enter — try: "methods for cluttered scenes with parallel-jaw grippers."
  4. Read the ranked methods + proof + evidence the app returns.

The shared header carries the product name, page navigation, a settings button, and a status bar that shows Computing… during a re-cluster, the active filter count, and how many methods are highlighted. Graph Reasoning is the default landing page; Explorer is optional (off by default). Use the diagram below to orient yourself across the four views.

Graph Reasoning — the default landing view

Type a question and the app detects intent, traverses the knowledge graph, and renders a "How we found your answer" page: an AnswerBlock (best methods + synthesis), a focused knowledge subgraph, a proof section (reasoning steps tied to graph edges), paper evidence filterable by rhetorical role (what the passage is doing in the paper — proposing an algorithm, describing setup, reporting a result, comparing, or stating a limitation, so you can jump straight to, e.g., only the results paragraphs) and by content type (prose vs. equation vs. table), plus equations and mined contradictions.

Usage example  Query "methods for cluttered scenes with parallel-jaw grippers." The graph walk surfaces Contact-GraspNet, DexDiffuser, and AnyGrasp; they jump to the top of the MethodTable, gain blue borders in the scatter, and brighten in the network. The evidence panel shows five passages from the Contact-GraspNet paper on scene difficulty and gripper type, with your query keywords inline-highlighted.

Benchmarks

Three tabs ground performance claims in the papers: Leaderboards (bar chart + ranked table per metric, with grade badges, CV%, and expandable source crops), Head-to-Head (win/loss records from same-paper comparisons), and Cross-Paper Validation (consistency cards across independent papers). Toggle Show low-confidence to include grade C; click any row to highlight it in the scatter.

Usage example  Pick "Success Rate (%) — real"; a grade-A entry such as 6-DoF GraspNet ranks near the top (e.g. score 90, with a low CV% computed across the 2+ independent papers that report it). Click Source to expand the row and read the exact caption "Table 1. Grasping results in real world experiments." (page 8) beside a cropped image of the actual cell showing "90%". (A grade-B, single-paper entry shows no CV%, since CV needs at least two independent reports — see Section 3.)

Explorer — optional

An interactive knowledge-graph dashboard with a KG filter panel (name search, edge-group toggles, min-connections slider, year range) and a gap matrix showing which (attribute A × attribute B) intersections have no methods — i.e., open research problems.

Usage example  In the gap matrix, the cell (transparent objects × tactile sensing) is empty → no method in the corpus covers that combination — a candidate research gap. Or, in the KG filter: search "GraspNet", toggle off all edge groups except Methodology, and raise the min-connections slider to hide isolated nodes, leaving only well-connected methods that share a backbone.

Admin — token-gated

A 3-step wizard manages domains: (1) Describe — upload CSV + domain description; (2) Configure — a language model proposes a YAML config mapping columns to roles (identity.name, method.family, …) and a claim-extraction focus; (3) Review — confirm and trigger the build. It shows live pipeline status and Vercel deployment progress.

Usage example  Click + New Domain, upload grasp_learning.csv and a papers.zip, accept the proposed column roles, add a claim focus ("grasping success rates on benchmark objects"), and click Create Domain — the build runs end-to-end and Vercel deploys the new route.

3Key Components & Features

Cross-highlighting

One selection state ties every view together. Selecting a method in the MethodTable highlights it in the scatter, network, and cluster graph and opens the DetailPanel; hovering a row spotlights its node in red across all charts. Query results highlight their methods the same way. Dots are sized by state (hovered 20px → highlighted 16px → filtered 12px → default 10px); unhighlighted methods fade to 35% opacity.

Re-clustering by weights & filters

UMAP weight sliders (Planning Method, Training Data, Hardware, …) re-prioritize dimensions; the AI can adjust them automatically, marking changed sliders in gold. Filtering the table to 3+ methods re-clusters that subset so a focused neighborhood re-layouts on its own.

Evidence grades & CV consistency

Every benchmark entry is graded for trust. CV% is the coefficient of variation of a method's reported values across independent papers (multi-cell extractions from one paper are deduplicated to a single report, so a CV% is only shown when 2+ papers report the metric). Thresholds vary by metric type (rate 10%, time 20%, default 15%).

GradeMeaning
A2+ papers, consistent (CV under threshold), verified
BSingle paper, verified (no CV — needs 2+ reports)
CLow extraction confidence, or papers disagree / different setup

How to use a grade: treat A as directly comparable, B as single-source (verify before citing), and C as a lead to check manually via the Source crop — not a settled number.

Provenance & proof

Each benchmark value carries a full provenance chain: paper_id, original value string, raw metric text, condition, table caption, page, extractor (tei_table or docling_tables), and a PNG crop of the source table. Unsalvageable or unresolved extractions are quarantined and excluded from all analysis. On the reasoning side, each proof step is tied to the graph edges it came from, so hovering a step highlights the exact edges that support it.

Predicted relationships

The Predicted Relationships view overlays edges inferred by a heterogeneous graph neural network — a GraphSAGE encoder (PyG SAGEConv, auto-heterogenized with to_hetero(aggr='mean')) paired with a DistMult per-relation decoder that scores a candidate edge as sigmoid(sum(src × R_rel × tgt)). (In the pipeline this step and its folder are named "HGT", but the model is SAGEConv+DistMult, not a transformer, and has no attention.) A confidence slider (0.5–0.9) filters predictions in real time; in practice scores cluster around 0.55–0.73, so pushing the slider to its top empties the view — keep it in that effective band. Predicted edges render dashed, and "show existing" overlays observed edges at low opacity for a before/after view. Clicking a predicted edge opens its confidence, its semantic relevance % (how textually related the two methods' papers are, independent of the structural prediction), shared-context snippets, and a comparability table (a side-by-side of the two methods' attributes so you can judge whether the predicted link is meaningful) — useful for novelty assessment and finding non-obvious related work the papers never cite.

Usage example  After comparing Contact-GraspNet and AnyGrasp, switch to Predicted Relationships: the model links them via a uses_technique edge (both use PointNet++) even though neither cites the other.

4Implementation & Tech

Papers are parsed, embedded, graphed, and learned over offline; the dashboard reads only precomputed JSON, so the UI stays fast. The pipeline runs in GitHub Actions and deploys to Vercel.

Library / ServiceRole in the system
React 19 + ContextComponent composition and the shared selection / query / weight state that drives cross-highlighting
Plotly.jsScatter (UMAP similarity map), leaderboard bar charts, head-to-head and cross-validation visuals
Cytoscape.js (fCoSE)Interactive node-link knowledge-graph rendering with one-shot physics layout (no jitter)
VercelStatic SPA hosting + serverless /api functions; auto-deploys on push to main
GROBIDPrimary PDF→TEI-XML parser (TEI-XML = the XML schema GROBID emits): structured sections, bibliography, authors, citations, tables
DoclingTable/figure extraction (TableFormer model) for benchmark numbers, with page bbox (bounding-box) cell grounding
sentence-transformersall-MiniLM-L6-v2 (384-dim) embeddings for paper similarity and chunk retrieval
ChromaPersistent vector store (cosine) holding multi-level chunks for RAG retrieval
PyTorch Geometric (PyG)GraphSAGE (SAGEConv) heterogeneous encoder + DistMult decoder for link prediction; outputs confidence-scored predicted edges
NetworkXBuilds and serializes the knowledge graph (papers, methods, techniques, contributions, …)
Grasp Planning Explorer — COMPARE ecosystem · robot-manipulation.org User & Engineer Manual
